schools.summary: Ellicott sets the three guilds (Asaph of Gershon, Heman of Kohath, Jeduthun of Merari) as representing all Levi, and reads 'prophesy' as musical utterance under a spiritual influence, a divine gift.
Matthew Henry: 'prophesying' is singing praise, not foretelling; the singers are 'workmen' whose praise is real labour, aimed wholly at the glory of God.
Clarke and Wesley take 'prophesy' as accompanying instruments with prayer and singing the psalms of David; the 'captains of the host' are the princes David gathered, not military officers.
Gill notes the setting apart was done before the princes and chief priests, the court before whom the lots for priests, Levites, and now singers were cast.

themes: the glory of God in temple music

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil read 'prophesied' as making music under direction; 'at the hand of Asaph' means only 'under the direction of'; Asarelah = Jesharelah (Ewald: 'Israel').

Clarke glosses 'prophesied' as singing hymns and praying, but reports the Targum and Jarchi's literal 'prophesied by the Holy Spirit' (cf. Elisha, 2 Kings 3:15); Wesley: under Asaph's and the king's direction.
Gill: the sons of Asaph were separated and by lot appointed to the singers' service.

debates.question: What does 'prophesy' mean in the temple music?

sides.position: the inspired art of sacred song, praise under a spiritual influence, not foretelling
literal prophecy, the Spirit of prophecy resting on the players as on Elisha
holders: Targum and Jarchi (as reported by Clarke)

schools.summary: Clarke and Wesley reckon the 'six' as five sons plus their father Jeduthun; Wesley allows the alternative that Shimei (v.17) is the missing sixth.
Ellicott and Keil hold that a name (Shimei) has dropped out, the one lot-name absent from vv.2-4; Ellicott also corrects Zeri to Izri and notes Jeshaiah = Isaiah.
Gill identifies Jeduthun with Ethan and names his sons.

debates.question: Why does the text say 'six' when only five sons of Jeduthun are named?

sides.position: the father Jeduthun is counted in with his five sons
a sixth name, Shimei, has dropped from the text and must be restored

schools.summary: Ellicott finds the last six names (minus Joshbekashah) forming a couplet fit for a seer, 'I have exalted the help; I have spoken abundance of visions,' but perhaps only a mnemonic; Keil reports Ewald's bolder claim of a prophet's oracle cut up into names. Ellicott also matches Uzziel=Azareel, Shebuel=Shubael.
Gill names Heman simply as the third chief singer whose sons these are.

debates.question: Do the last names of Heman's sons form a real prayer or oracle?

sides.position: yes, a genuine prophetic utterance woven into the family names
possibly only a mnemonic arrangement of ordinary names that happens to fall into verse

themes: the names that read as a prayer

schools.summary: Ellicott: Heman is 'the king's seer' (hozeh) in liturgical matters, music and prophecy standing close (1 Sam 10:5; 2 Kings 3:15); 'lift up the horn' is a Hebrew metaphor (1 Sam 2:10) for God strengthening him with able 'sons'; the three daughters show women shared the song. Keil: 'all these' spans all three houses.

Clarke (with Jarchi) takes 'lift up the horn' literally, the horn of prophecy sounded like a trumpet; Wesley hears a martial instrument suited to David's spirit; both note the daughters among the singers.
Gill names Heman the prophet of the king, frequently with him.

debates.question: What is meant by 'to lift up the horn'?

sides.position: a literal horn or trumpet sounded in prophecy
the Hebrew metaphor 'lift up the horn' — God strengthened Heman with able assistants

themes: women in the service of song

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'all these' are the 24 leaders (vv.2-4), each under his own 'father'/director; renders the close 'under the king, and Asaph, Heman, Jeduthun' — the arrangement made by David with his three chief musicians, as the priests were classified with Zadok and Ahimelech (1 Chr 24:3).
Gill: the singers were taught and directed by their father in the temple service.

themes: the service ordered from the throne

schools.summary: Ellicott reads 288 as 24x12, each of the 24 leaders with eleven expert brethren; Keil weighs the 'ward against ward' grammar, binding it to the lots rather than 'class like class.'
Clarke: 288 = twelve classes of 24; Wesley: the 'cunning' could teach others, and with their scholars made up the 4,000 of 1 Chr 23:5.
Gill: each chief singer had twelve with him, himself and eleven more.
Matthew Henry: the lot was cast to prevent strife for precedency, levelling small and great, teacher and scholar, and referring all to God who goes not by our rules (Matt 20:23).

Ellicott: 'lots of charge' fixing the order of the guilds (cf. the priestly course, Luke 1:6); the younger houses had no advantage; 'talmid' (scholar) occurs only here in the OT, the Talmud's word for a disciple; 288 masters trained ~3,700 more (1 Chr 23:5).
Gill: 'ward against ward' fixes which course of singers attends which course of priests in the weekly service.
Wesley: the ward is a course of Levites answering to a priest (1 Chr 23:28), the lot drawn without respect to age or ability.

themes: small and great equal before God

schools.summary: Ellicott supplies the refrain 'he, his sons, brethren, twelve' required by the 288; Keil argues all the lots were in one urn, so only Heman's sons remained after the fourteenth drawing; 'brethren' = clansmen, 'sons' = disciples.
Gill: the first lot fell to Joseph, Asaph's second son, before the firstborn, the lot ignoring birth-order from the start.
Wesley: the clause 'he, his sons, and his brethren were twelve' must be understood throughout, else the 288 fails; Clarke calls Joseph and Gedaliah firstborns.

debates.question: Was Joseph the firstborn or the second son of Asaph?

sides.position: Asaph's second son, the younger preferred before the firstborn

schools.summary: Keil heads a block carrying the count through the middle lots; Ellicott fixes the repeating form 'the third, Zaccur... twelve,' and reads 'the Izrite' (v.11) as a Gentilic, so these are clans, not bare individuals.
Clarke: Izri is the second son of Jeduthun (the Zeri of v.3).
Ellicott: Zeri and Izri are one, an interchange as old as the versions, the form being 'the Izrite,' a family name; Keil's pattern places this Jeduthunite at an even lot, the fourth.

themes: the harp for thanks and praise

schools.summary: Clarke: Nethaniah is Asaph's third son, and 'thus the lot did not run in any particular order.'
Ellicott keeps the steady count 'the fifth, Nethaniah... twelve.'

themes: the lot in no fixed order
the hidden choice of the LORD

schools.summary: Ellicott anchors Jesharelah in Asaph's house (the Asarelah of v.2, Ewald's 'Israel'), the youngest of Asaph's four, drawing the seventh lot.
Clarke supposes Jesharelah the same as Uzziel, a son of Heman, an identification the other witness resists.

outliers.note: Identifies Jesharelah with Uzziel of Heman's house, against Ellicott's placing of the name in Asaph's line.

themes: a name worn smooth in copying

schools.summary: Ellicott: Shimei is the name 'omitted by accident from v.3,' the one lot-name (vv.9-31) absent from the family roll (vv.2-4), now restored at the tenth course; the Alexandrian Greek even reinserts him in v.3.
Ellicott matches Azareel with the Uzziel of v.4 (a one-letter difference, like Azariah/Uzziah, 1 Chr 3:12; 2 Chr 26:1), the same singer under two spellings.
Ellicott notes only 'so the Hebrew,' the name Hashabiah standing without difficulty; Keil's pattern places this Jeduthunite at an even lot, the twelfth.

themes: silence where the text is clear

schools.summary: Ellicott construes the Hebrew idiom 'as to the thirteenth, Shubael...' that governs the rest of the list, and identifies Shubael with the Shebuel of v.4 and the Levitical lists (1 Chr 23:16; 24:20).

themes: the register as a working document

schools.summary: Ellicott notes the spelling wavers between Jeremoth and Jerimoth (v.4; cf. 1 Chr 23:23; 24:30) across the LXX, Vulgate, Syriac, and Arabic; Keil's single-urn reading explains why every lot from the fifteenth falls to Heman's house.

themes: Heman's house fills the later lots

schools.summary: Ellicott marks Joshbekashah as the 'peculiar' name that will not fit the supposed prayer of Heman's sons, evidence on his caution that the couplet may be mnemonic, not oracle; Keil: the late lots fell to Heman's large house from the single urn.
Matthew Henry sets Joshbekashah among Heman's sons and his lot among the four-and-twenty assigned by the impartial lot.

themes: the name that breaks the prayer

schools.summary: Ellicott: Mahazioth ('visions') supplies the closing phrase of the couplet, 'I have spoken abundance of visions'; Keil reports Ewald's oracle 'I have to fulness spoken oracles'; the late lot falls to Heman's house from the single urn.
Matthew Henry reckons Mahazioth among Heman's fourteen sons, his lot assigned by no rule of age.

themes: a syllable of the buried prayer

schools.summary: Ellicott analyses the whole distribution (Asaph's four odd lots, Jeduthun's six, and all the rest to Heman's fourteen), proving a single urn; Keil concurs against Bertheau's separate urns.
Clarke fixes Romamti-ezer as one name (v.4), then dismisses the whole institution: 'with this immense parade of noise and show (David's own invention), Christianity has nothing to do.'
Matthew Henry (from the chapter's opening) holds that bringing such instrumental concerts into worship now is what none pretend to; the music belonged to that dispensation.

debates.question: Was the temple's instrumental music a divine institution or David's own invention?

sides.position: a divinely ordered service, described without censure
belonging only to that dispensation, not carried into the gospel church (Clarke laying it to David's own invention)

outliers.note: Breaks off to disparage the whole apparatus of temple music as 'David's own invention' with which 'Christianity has nothing to do' — the sharpest dissent in the chapter.
